pattern: Conﬂ uent white matter lesions in an adult

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is confluent, asymmetric areas of abnormal T2 and FLAIR hyperintensity within the subcortical and deep periventricular white matter. These lesions typically involve the subcortical U-fibers while characteristically sparing the overlying cerebral cortex.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include a striking lack of significant mass effect or architectural distortion, which is out of proportion to the large size of the white matter lesions. Additionally, these regions typically demonstrate no significant contrast enhancement, though faint peripheral enhancement can rarely occur.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of significant mass effect, midline shift, or prominent vasogenic oedema. Furthermore, there is no discrete nodular or irregular ring enhancement to suggest a high-grade neoplasm or infectious abscess.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of extensive, confluent white matter signal abnormality that completely lacks both mass effect and contrast enhancement in an adult.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (patient with human immunodeﬁ ciency virus), given the extensive, asymmetric white matter involvement sparing the cortex, without associated mass effect or enhancement. This demyelinating disease is caused by the JC virus in immunocompromised patients, classically presenting with progressive focal neurological deficits.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Demyelinating disease, however acute demyelinating plaques in conditions like multiple sclerosis typically demonstrate transient contrast enhancement and tend to be more discrete, ovoid lesions perpendicular to the ventricles rather than large confluent asymmetrical swathes. Furthermore, tumefactive demyelination usually presents with at least some degree of mass effect and incomplete ring enhancement.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Neoplasm, given that an infiltrating primary tumour such as gliomatosis cerebri or primary CNS lymphoma can also present with extensive white matter signal change. Missing a neoplastic process could significantly delay targeted oncological therapy, although these tumours typically exert mass effect and, in the case of lymphoma, usually demonstrate avid enhancement.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the full extent of white matter involvement and confidently rule out occult mass effect or focal enhancing lesions that might mandate a biopsy.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to evaluate the tempo of white matter destruction, which in PML is typically rapidly progressive, and to determine the patient's baseline if there is a known history of HIV or immunosuppression.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the true extent of white matter disease, demonstrating the typical sparing of the cortical ribbon, and definitively excluding subtle pathological contrast enhancement.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2 and FLAIR sequences to map the demyelination, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) as active PML edges may show restricted diffusion, and pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to rigorously evaluate for any subtle areas of enhancement.
management.intervention: If the clinical picture and imaging remain indeterminate or a neoplasm cannot be excluded, I would specify a referral for cerebrospinal fluid sampling via lumbar puncture (to test for JC virus PCR) or an image-guided stereotactic brain biopsy to obtain a definitive tissue or viral diagnosis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of the white matter lesions, the lack of mass effect, and the absence of enhancement to support the clinical diagnosis of PML and guide appropriate adjustment of immunosuppressive or antiretroviral therapies.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is confluent, asymmetric areas of abnormal T2 and FLAIR hyperintensity within the subcortical and deep periventricular white matter. These lesions typically involve the subcortical U-fibers while characteristically sparing the overlying cerebral cortex. Supporting features include a striking lack of significant mass effect or architectural distortion, which is out of proportion to the large size of the white matter lesions. Additionally, these regions typically demonstrate no significant contrast enhancement, though faint peripheral enhancement can rarely occur. Importantly, there is no evidence of significant mass effect, midline shift, or prominent vasogenic oedema. Furthermore, there is no discrete nodular or irregular ring enhancement to suggest a high-grade neoplasm or infectious abscess. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of extensive, confluent white matter signal abnormality that completely lacks both mass effect and contrast enhancement in an adult.

The appearances are most in keeping with Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (patient with human immunodeﬁ ciency virus), given the extensive, asymmetric white matter involvement sparing the cortex, without associated mass effect or enhancement. This demyelinating disease is caused by the JC virus in immunocompromised patients, classically presenting with progressive focal neurological deficits. The principle differential would be Demyelinating disease, however acute demyelinating plaques in conditions like multiple sclerosis typically demonstrate transient contrast enhancement and tend to be more discrete, ovoid lesions perpendicular to the ventricles rather than large confluent asymmetrical swathes. Furthermore, tumefactive demyelination usually presents with at least some degree of mass effect and incomplete ring enhancement. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Neoplasm, given that an infiltrating primary tumour such as gliomatosis cerebri or primary CNS lymphoma can also present with extensive white matter signal change. Missing a neoplastic process could significantly delay targeted oncological therapy, although these tumours typically exert mass effect and, in the case of lymphoma, usually demonstrate avid enhancement.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the full extent of white matter involvement and confidently rule out occult mass effect or focal enhancing lesions that might mandate a biopsy. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to evaluate the tempo of white matter destruction, which in PML is typically rapidly progressive, and to determine the patient's baseline if there is a known history of HIV or immunosuppression. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain if only a CT has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the true extent of white matter disease, demonstrating the typical sparing of the cortical ribbon, and definitively excluding subtle pathological contrast enhancement. I would specify high-resolution T2 and FLAIR sequences to map the demyelination, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) as active PML edges may show restricted diffusion, and pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to rigorously evaluate for any subtle areas of enhancement. If the clinical picture and imaging remain indeterminate or a neoplasm cannot be excluded, I would specify a referral for cerebrospinal fluid sampling via lumbar puncture (to test for JC virus PCR) or an image-guided stereotactic brain biopsy to obtain a definitive tissue or viral diagnosis. This should go to the Neurology and Infectious Diseases MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of the white matter lesions, the lack of mass effect, and the absence of enhancement to support the clinical diagnosis of PML and guide appropriate adjustment of immunosuppressive or antiretroviral therapies.
